title: Designing Robust CI/CD Pipelines

seo.title: Designing Robust CI/CD Pipelines | Baalvion
seo.description: Pipeline stages, test strategy, in-line security scanning, progressive delivery and rollback — how to design a CI/CD pipeline you can trust at platform scale.

author: Baalvion Engineering
excerpt: A robust CI/CD pipeline is an opinionated assembly line: build once, prove it everywhere, ship it progressively, revert in one action. How Baalvion designs pipelines money-moving systems trust.

blocks.text: A pipeline is a contract, not a convenience

The point of a continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipeline is not to automate the steps an engineer used to run by hand. It is to encode a contract: nothing reaches production without passing the gates the organisation has agreed are non-negotiable. Speed is a by-product of that discipline, not the goal of it — a pipeline that ships fast but lets an unscanned artefact or an untested migration through has not saved time, it has deferred a failure to a worse moment. At Baalvion Industries we run the Baalvion Operating System (BOS) as a multi-tenant platform across 198 markets and 180+ jurisdictions, with five layers — Infrastructure, Intelligence, Governance, Commerce and Finance — sharing one control plane. When a single pipeline can promote a change that touches cross-border settlement, a regulatory filing path and a luxury-commerce checkout, the pipeline is where correctness, security and compliance are enforced or lost.

The throughline is that a good pipeline is opinionated. It builds an artefact exactly once and refuses to rebuild it per environment. It runs tests in a deliberate shape rather than throwing everything at every commit. It treats security scanning as a gate, not a report. It releases progressively so the blast radius is small, and it makes rollback a first-class, one-action capability rather than a forensic exercise. Each of these is a design decision with trade-offs, and the sections below are honest about them. The complementary operating-model view sits in our companion piece on [DevOps best practices](/news/tech/devops-best-practices); here we go deep on the pipeline itself.
Stages: build once, promote unchanged

The structural backbone of a trustworthy pipeline is a single build stage that produces one immutable, content-addressed artefact — a container image identified by digest, not by a mutable tag like 'latest'. That artefact is then promoted unchanged from one environment to the next: integration, staging, production. The rule that the artefact is never rebuilt between environments sounds pedantic until you internalise the alternative — a rebuild is, by definition, a different artefact, and therefore one your staging tests never actually validated. Environments differ only by injected configuration and secrets, never by recompilation. This is the most common design flaw we see in pipelines that 'work' but cannot be trusted: they re-run the build per stage and quietly break the chain of evidence between what was tested and what is running.

A clean stage layout for a BOS service is a sequence of widening confidence: the earliest stages are cheap and fast so they fail loudly within minutes, while later stages are expensive and run only on artefacts that have already earned their place. Crucially, the artefact carries provenance with it — a signed attestation and a software bill of materials (SBOM) generated at build time — so every later gate, and every auditor afterward, can answer 'what is in this thing and who built it' without guesswork. For a compliance-first platform under SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001, that provenance is the audit trail, not decoration.

items: Source and dependency resolution with a locked dependency graph, so the build is reproducible and the SBOM is accurate.
Compile and unit-test stage — the fast feedback loop that should complete in minutes and block on the first failure.
Build the single immutable artefact, sign it, and emit an SBOM and build-provenance attestation alongside it.
Integration and contract tests against the real artefact and ephemeral dependencies, never against a separately rebuilt copy.
Security and policy gates that can block promotion of the artefact, not merely annotate it.
Progressive release to production with automated health verification and an armed rollback path.

blocks.text: Testing: shape the pyramid, protect the loop

Test strategy inside a pipeline is a question of shape, not volume. The test pyramid remains the right default: a broad base of fast unit tests, a narrower band of integration and contract tests, and a deliberately thin cap of end-to-end checks. The reason is economic. Unit tests give the tightest feedback loop and the most precise failure localisation; end-to-end tests are slow, environmentally fragile and notoriously flaky, so an inverted pyramid that leans on them destroys exactly the rapid feedback the pipeline exists to provide. The discipline is to push each test down to the lowest layer that can meaningfully verify the behaviour, and reserve full end-to-end runs for the handful of flows where cross-service interaction is the actual risk.

Two practices keep the test stage honest at platform scale. First, contract tests between services — consumer-driven contracts in the style of Pact — catch integration breakage at the boundary without standing up the whole system, which matters when a settlement service is consumed by a dozen other layers. Second, treat flakiness as a defect with the same severity as a logic bug: a non-deterministic test engineers learn to re-run on red is worse than no test, because it teaches the team to ignore the gate. We quarantine flaky tests and fix or delete them, because a pipeline people route around is not a gate. Test data is its own design problem — ephemeral, isolated databases per run, seeded deterministically, prevent the cross-contamination that makes 'works on my run' a nightmare. This is part of how we approach [secure software development](/news/tech/secure-software-development): correctness and security are tested in the same loop, not bolted on afterward.
Security scanning: shift left into the gate

Security in a pipeline is the difference between scanning that produces a report someone reads later and scanning that blocks a release now. The latter is the only kind that changes outcomes. A well-designed pipeline layers several scans, each catching a different class of problem, and each empowered to fail the build. Static application security testing (SAST) reads the source for injection flaws, unsafe deserialisation and the like. Software composition analysis (SCA) inspects the dependency tree against vulnerability databases — the dominant source of real-world breaches is a known CVE in a transitive dependency, not novel first-party code. Secret scanning catches credentials committed by accident before they reach a registry. Container image scanning checks the assembled artefact for vulnerable OS packages. For platforms with running infrastructure as code, policy-as-code tools such as OPA or Sentinel evaluate the planned infrastructure change against guardrails before it is applied.

The hard decisions here are about thresholds and supply chain. Blocking on every finding is paralysis; blocking on nothing is theatre. The workable policy is risk-tiered: critical and high-severity findings with a known exploit path block promotion, while lower-severity findings open a tracked, time-boxed exception rather than a silent pass. On the supply-chain side, signing artefacts (with Sigstore/cosign or equivalent) and verifying those signatures at deploy time proves the thing being deployed is the thing that was built and scanned, defending against the tampering supply-chain attacks rely on. For BOS, where artefacts handle KYC/AML data under AES-256 encryption and GDPR obligations, an unsigned or unscanned image reaching production is not a deployment; it is a reportable incident. This is the same discipline we bring to our enterprise [DevOps](/services/devops) and [cloud solutions](/services/cloud-solutions) practices.
Progressive delivery: shrink the blast radius

A pipeline that promotes a change to one hundred percent of traffic in a single step has built a very efficient way to cause a very large outage. Progressive delivery is the design response: decouple deployment from release, and expose new code to a widening slice of traffic while watching it. The foundational technique is the separation itself — ship the artefact to production dark behind a feature flag, so it is deployed but not yet serving anyone, which means a problem can be switched off without a redeploy. On top of that, canary releases route a small percentage of live traffic to the new version, hold it there while automated analysis compares its error rate, latency and saturation against the stable version, and proceed only if the signals stay healthy. Blue-green deployments keep two full environments and cut traffic over atomically, trading higher infrastructure cost for an instant, clean switch and an equally instant switch back.

The trade-offs are real. Canary analysis is only as good as its metrics and bake time — too short a soak and you promote a slow-burning regression; too long and you throttle delivery. It demands genuine observability: high-cardinality telemetry tagged by tenant, jurisdiction and transaction type, so a canary failing for one region is visible rather than averaged away. Feature flags accumulate as technical debt if never retired, so each flag needs an owner and an expiry from day one. Progressive delivery also forces backward compatibility on the data layer — canary and stable run simultaneously against the same schema, so migrations must be expand-then-contract rather than breaking. Tools like Argo Rollouts and Flagger automate the canary loop on top of GitOps, the model we use because it keeps the desired state declarative, reviewed and revertible — the foundation of the auditable delivery our Governance layer requires.
Rollback: design for the bad day first

The single most underrated property of a pipeline is how fast and how safely it can undo a release. Rollback should be a first-class, one-action capability designed before the happy path, because the day you need it is the day you have no time to invent it. With immutable artefacts and GitOps, the cleanest rollback is a revert of the deployment commit: the previous known-good artefact is already built, signed and scanned, so re-promoting it carries no rebuild risk. Progressive delivery makes rollback cheaper still — if the change is behind a flag you turn the flag off; if it is a canary you abort the rollout and traffic returns to stable automatically. The principle is that recovery must not depend on the same humans who are mid-incident reconstructing what to do.

The genuinely hard part of rollback is state, not code. Rolling back a stateless service is trivial; rolling back after a database migration or after the new version has written data in a new shape is not. This is why migrations must be backward-compatible by design — additive changes deployed and verified before any destructive cleanup — so that reverting the application does not corrupt or orphan data. For money-moving operations the stakes are sharper still: a retried or rolled-back transfer must not double-execute, which makes idempotency keys a correctness requirement rather than a nicety. On BOS, where a duplicated cross-border settlement is a financial incident and not a glitch, every write path a rollback or retry could touch is idempotent by construction. That is the rigour we mean by infrastructure-grade, and it is what we bring to the [enterprise software](/services/enterprise-software) we build for others.
Putting it together at platform scale

None of these decisions is independent. Build-once-promote-unchanged makes a fast rollback trustworthy, because the artefact you revert to was genuinely the one you tested. A well-shaped test pyramid keeps the early gates fast enough that engineers integrate frequently. In-line scanning only works if the artefact carries provenance from the build stage; progressive delivery only works if observability is rich enough to judge a canary; rollback only works cheaply if migrations were designed to be reversible. Designed together they form a delivery substrate a small platform team can offer as a paved road — golden pipelines, signed images, GitOps promotion, canary analysis and armed rollback — and that is how the Baalvion Operating System absorbs 500K+ transactions and serves 125+ active partners across our [platform](/platform) while meeting SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001 and GDPR. If you are designing or hardening a pipeline of your own, our [technology consulting](/services/technology-consulting) team starts from the same questions this article does: what must this pipeline refuse to ship, how small can the blast radius be, and how fast can a bad day be undone — not which tool to buy first.

faqs.q: What is the difference between continuous integration, delivery and deployment?
faqs.a: Continuous integration means every change merges to the mainline frequently behind an automated build-and-test gate, keeping the integration problem small. Continuous delivery extends that gate to produce a production-ready artefact on every commit, releasable at the push of a button. Continuous deployment removes the human approval and ships automatically once gates pass. The choice between delivery and deployment is a risk decision — high-stakes, money-moving paths often keep an explicit approval.

faqs.q: Why is building the artefact only once so important?
faqs.a: Because a rebuild is a different artefact than the one your tests validated. If you recompile per environment, the chain of evidence between what was tested in staging and what runs in production is broken. Building one immutable, content-addressed artefact and promoting it unchanged — with only configuration injected per environment — is what makes the whole pipeline, including rollback, trustworthy.

faqs.q: How should security scanning be wired into a pipeline?
faqs.a: As a gate, not a report. Layer SAST for source flaws, software composition analysis for vulnerable dependencies, secret scanning, and container image scanning, and give each the authority to block promotion. Use risk-tiered thresholds — critical findings block, lower-severity ones open tracked, time-boxed exceptions. Sign artefacts and verify the signature at deploy time so you prove the deployed thing is the thing that was built and scanned.

faqs.q: What is progressive delivery and when do you need it?
faqs.a: Progressive delivery decouples deployment from release and exposes new code to a widening slice of traffic — via feature flags, canary releases or blue-green cutover — while automated analysis watches error rate, latency and saturation. You need it once an outage's blast radius is unacceptable, which for most production systems is early. It demands genuine observability and backward-compatible database migrations so old and new versions can run side by side.

faqs.q: Why is rollback harder than it looks, and how do you design for it?
faqs.a: Rolling back stateless code is trivial; rolling back state is not. If the new version ran a breaking migration or wrote data in a new shape, reverting the application can corrupt or orphan data. The fix is to make migrations backward-compatible (expand-then-contract) and make money-moving write paths idempotent so retries and reverts never double-execute. Treat rollback as a first-class, one-action capability designed before the happy path.

faqs.q: How does Baalvion run CI/CD for the Baalvion Operating System?
faqs.a: BOS ships on GitOps-driven, build-once-promote-unchanged pipelines with signed immutable artefacts, in-pipeline SAST/SCA/secret/image scanning that blocks on critical findings, OpenTelemetry-based observability, canary-based progressive delivery via Argo Rollouts, and idempotent write paths with backward-compatible migrations for instant rollback. That discipline lets a multi-tenant platform serving 198 markets meet SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001 and GDPR while sustaining 500K+ transactions.
